tagline: The guardian of your DNA; broken in roughly half of all human cancers
function_friendly: TP53 — usually just called "p53" — is the cell's emergency brake. When DNA gets damaged or a cell starts dividing out of control, p53 either halts the cell, sends it for repair, or orders it to self-destruct before it can become cancerous. About half of all human cancers have a broken or missing p53 gene, which is why it's called "the guardian of the genome." Restoring p53 function has been one of the longest-running goals in cancer drug development.

tagline: The breast and ovarian cancer risk gene Angelina Jolie made famous
function_friendly: BRCA1 is a DNA-repair foreman that fixes one of the most dangerous kinds of damage — both strands of the DNA double helix breaking at the same time. People who inherit a broken copy of BRCA1 carry a much higher lifetime risk of breast and ovarian cancer, which is why preventive screening and surgery exist for carriers. The gene became a household name after Angelina Jolie disclosed her own BRCA1 mutation and preventive mastectomy in 2013. A drug class called PARP inhibitors (olaparib, talazoparib) works especially well against BRCA1-driven cancers.

tagline: BRCA1's lesser-known sibling, equally serious when broken
function_friendly: BRCA2 does the same job as BRCA1 — repairing double-strand breaks in DNA — and inherited mutations in it raise the same kinds of cancer risk, especially breast, ovarian, prostate, and pancreatic. It gets less public attention than BRCA1 but is equally important medically. The same PARP-inhibitor drugs that target BRCA1 cancers also work against BRCA2-driven tumors, an approach that exploits the inability of cancer cells to repair their own DNA damage.

tagline: A growth switch on cell surfaces; target of cancer pills like Tarceva
function_friendly: EGFR is a growth switch that sits on the outside surface of cells. When a signaling molecule docks onto it, EGFR tells the cell to grow and divide. Many lung and colon cancers carry a stuck-on EGFR that drives uncontrolled growth, which is why drugs that block it (Tarceva, Iressa, cetuximab, osimertinib) are mainstays of targeted cancer therapy. EGFR is also closely related to ERBB2/HER2, and the two often work as partners on cell surfaces.

tagline: Better known as HER2; the breast cancer target of Herceptin
function_friendly: ERBB2 — known clinically as HER2 — is a close cousin of EGFR and a partner in many of the same signaling circuits. In about one in five breast cancers it's amplified or overactive, driving aggressive tumor growth. The antibody drug trastuzumab (Herceptin) revolutionized treatment of HER2-positive breast cancer in the late 1990s, and a newer class of antibody-drug conjugates (Enhertu) extends the benefit further. HER2 testing is now standard at breast cancer diagnosis.

tagline: The most-mutated cancer gene in humans; long called "undruggable"
function_friendly: KRAS is one of the cell's main "on/off" switches for growth signals — a small protein that flips between an active and inactive state. When KRAS gets stuck "on" through mutation, the cell receives a permanent growth signal. About a quarter of all human cancers carry a KRAS mutation, especially pancreatic, colon, and lung tumors. For decades KRAS was called "undruggable," but recent drugs (sotorasib, adagrasib) finally crack one specific variant called KRAS G12C.

tagline: A signaling switch broken in most melanomas
function_friendly: BRAF passes growth signals down a chain inside cells. A single mutation (V600E) gets it stuck in the "on" position, and that exact mutation is found in most melanomas plus a fraction of colon and thyroid cancers. Drugs that block mutant BRAF (vemurafenib, dabrafenib) shrink melanomas dramatically — though tumors often find a way around them, which is why modern treatment combines a BRAF inhibitor with a MEK inhibitor that hits the downstream step.

tagline: Fused into BCR-ABL in chronic myeloid leukemia; the first targeted-therapy success (Gleevec)
function_friendly: ABL1 is normally a quiet signaling enzyme, but in chronic myeloid leukemia a piece of one chromosome fuses to a piece of another to form BCR-ABL — a permanently active growth signal. In 2001, imatinib (Gleevec) became the first targeted cancer therapy by blocking exactly that fusion protein, turning a previously fatal leukemia into a manageable chronic disease. The success of Gleevec launched the entire era of precision oncology and inspired hundreds of drugs that have followed.

tagline: A rare lung-cancer driver with its own family of targeted drugs
function_friendly: ALK is a signaling switch normally active during embryonic development and quiet in adults. In a small slice of lung cancers (~5%) and certain lymphomas, ALK fuses with another gene to form a permanently active version that drives the tumor. Drugs that block fused ALK (crizotinib, alectinib, lorlatinib) have transformed treatment for those patients, often producing dramatic and lasting responses. New ALK inhibitors are designed specifically to penetrate the brain, where ALK-positive lung tumors frequently spread.

tagline: The universal oncogene; pushed up in cancers across the body
function_friendly: MYC is a master regulator that tells cells when to grow and divide. It's pushed to abnormally high levels in most human cancers, making it perhaps the most universal cancer driver. MYC itself has been notoriously hard to target with drugs — it's a fast-moving regulatory protein with no obvious binding pocket — and drugging it remains one of cancer biology's great unsolved problems. Most current efforts try to block MYC indirectly by interfering with the proteins that switch it on.

tagline: A tumor suppressor frequently lost in prostate, brain, and breast cancer
function_friendly: PTEN is one of the cell's main growth brakes — it counteracts the growth signals coming through the PI3K pathway. When PTEN is lost or mutated, growth signaling runs unchecked and cells divide too readily. PTEN deletions are common across many cancers but especially in prostate, glioblastoma, and breast tumors. People who inherit a broken PTEN gene develop Cowden syndrome, an inherited condition that raises lifetime risk of multiple cancers and causes benign skin and gut growths.

tagline: Loss of this protein starts most colorectal cancers
function_friendly: APC is a brake on intestinal cell growth. People born with one broken APC gene develop hundreds or thousands of colon polyps starting in their teens — a condition called familial adenomatous polyposis that nearly always becomes cancer without preventive colon removal. Beyond inherited disease, sporadic loss of APC is the very first step in the great majority of sporadic colorectal cancers, making it the textbook example of a "gatekeeper" tumor suppressor.

tagline: The first tumor suppressor ever discovered, named for the childhood eye cancer retinoblastoma
function_friendly: RB1 was the first tumor suppressor ever discovered, found because broken inheritance of it causes retinoblastoma — a rare childhood eye cancer. Its job is to hold the cell cycle paused until proper growth signals arrive to release the brake. Loss of RB1 contributes to many adult cancers as well, including small-cell lung and bladder cancer, and it remains the prototype "guardian of the dividing cell." Modern CDK4/6 inhibitor drugs work on the same circuit upstream of RB1.

tagline: Tells tumors to grow blood vessels; blocked by Avastin
function_friendly: VEGFA is the molecular signal that tells the body to grow new blood vessels. Tumors hijack it to get oxygen and nutrients delivered through new capillaries, and blocking VEGFA starves them. Bevacizumab (Avastin) is the most famous anti-VEGFA antibody, used in colon, lung, kidney, and brain cancers. VEGFA biology also explains common side effects of these drugs — high blood pressure, impaired wound healing, and rarely, bleeding — since healthy tissues also rely on it.

tagline: Better known as PD-L1; tumors wear it as a "don't eat me" sign
function_friendly: CD274 — almost always called PD-L1 — is a "don't kill me" signal that normal cells display to keep the immune system from attacking them. Many tumors learn to wear the same flag as a disguise. Drugs that block PD-L1 or its receptor PD-1 strip away the disguise and let T cells recognize the tumor again — the modern immunotherapy revolution. The level of PD-L1 a tumor wears often helps predict which patients will respond to these drugs.

tagline: The PD-1 checkpoint; target of Keytruda and Opdivo, the modern immunotherapy revolution
function_friendly: PDCD1 — the PD-1 protein — sits on the surface of T cells and acts as a built-in "off switch." Tumors exploit it by waving the PD-L1 flag (see CD274) to silence the T cells attacking them. Drugs that block PD-1 (pembrolizumab/Keytruda, nivolumab/Opdivo) re-awaken the immune system against cancer, and the discovery of this checkpoint won the 2018 Nobel Prize in Medicine. PD-1 inhibitors are now standard in melanoma, lung, kidney, Hodgkin lymphoma, and a growing list of others.

tagline: A DNA-repair foreman; broken in Lynch syndrome (hereditary colon cancer)
function_friendly: MLH1 is part of a DNA "spell-check" system that catches small typos made when DNA is copied. People born with one broken copy of MLH1 develop Lynch syndrome — a sharply raised lifetime risk of colon, endometrial, and several other cancers. Tumors missing MLH1 accumulate hundreds of mutations and are often unusually responsive to immunotherapy, because they generate many abnormal proteins for the immune system to recognize and attack.

tagline: One of the most commonly mutated genes in breast and endometrial cancer
function_friendly: PIK3CA encodes the active half of an enzyme called PI3-kinase that sits at the start of a major growth-signaling pathway downstream of receptors like EGFR. Mutations that switch it on are among the most common in breast cancer and endometrial cancer, and also appear in a developmental overgrowth disorder called PROS. Drugs that block mutant PI3K (such as alpelisib for hormone-receptor-positive breast cancer) selectively target tumors carrying PIK3CA mutations.

tagline: The p16 cell-cycle brake; broken in melanoma and pancreatic cancer
function_friendly: CDKN2A produces two key tumor-suppressor proteins from the same gene, including p16 — a brake that holds the cell cycle paused. The gene is frequently deleted in melanoma, pancreatic cancer, and certain brain tumors, and inherited mutations raise lifetime risk of multiple cancers. CDK4/6 inhibitor drugs (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) act on the same downstream circuit and are widely used in hormone-positive breast cancer, restoring some of the brake that lost p16 fails to apply.

tagline: The original "don't die" signal in cancer cells; target of venetoclax
function_friendly: BCL2 is a "don't die" signal — it stops cells from triggering their built-in self-destruct program (apoptosis). Cancer cells often crank up BCL2 to make themselves nearly immortal, an unusual trick because most cells die when they accumulate enough damage. Venetoclax, a drug that directly blocks BCL2, has transformed treatment of certain leukemias (CLL, AML) and lymphomas, often producing dramatic responses by re-enabling the suicide program these cancer cells had silenced.

tagline: Insulin — the hormone that tells your body what to do with sugar; first protein ever sequenced
function_friendly: Insulin is the hormone your pancreas releases after meals to tell your body what to do with sugar. It signals muscle, liver, and fat cells to pull sugar out of the blood and either burn it for energy or store it for later use. Type 1 diabetes happens when the immune system destroys the cells that make insulin; type 2 happens when the body stops responding to it properly. Insulin was the first protein ever sequenced (Sanger, 1953) and the first medicine ever made with genetic engineering (Genentech, 1982).

tagline: Insulin's docking station on the surface of liver, muscle, and fat cells
function_friendly: INSR is the docking station for insulin on the outer surface of muscle, fat, and liver cells. When insulin binds, INSR fires off a chain of signals inside the cell that open sugar gateways and switch metabolism toward energy use and storage. In type 2 diabetes, INSR and its downstream signals become unresponsive — the cells stop "hearing" insulin even though insulin is still being released, and blood sugar climbs.

tagline: Glucagon — insulin's opposite; tells the liver to release stored sugar
function_friendly: Glucagon is insulin's opposite — also made in the pancreas, but in a different cell type. When blood sugar drops too low between meals or during exercise, glucagon tells the liver to release its sugar stores back into the bloodstream. People with diabetes who take insulin can carry an emergency glucagon injection in case of severe hypoglycemia. Newer dual drugs that activate the glucagon receptor along with the GLP-1 receptor are being tested for obesity.

tagline: Leptin — the "I'm full" hormone made by your fat tissue
function_friendly: Leptin is a hormone made by fat tissue that reports back to the brain: "we have plenty of energy stored, you can stop eating." The more fat, the more leptin. Very rare mutations that knock out leptin cause severe early-onset obesity that recombinant leptin can treat. In ordinary obesity, however, leptin levels are already high and the brain has become "leptin-resistant" — which is why leptin itself never became a successful weight-loss drug.

tagline: Ghrelin — the "I'm hungry" hormone made by your empty stomach
function_friendly: Ghrelin is the "I'm hungry" hormone — produced by an empty stomach and dropping after a meal. It tells the brain to seek food and stimulates appetite. Ghrelin levels rise before scheduled mealtimes, which is part of why hunger feels timed to habit. Gastric bypass surgery reduces ghrelin levels and reduces hunger, which is one reason it works so durably for weight loss compared with dieting alone.

tagline: Breaks down phenylalanine; broken in PKU, the disease behind newborn heel-prick screening
function_friendly: PAH is an enzyme that breaks down phenylalanine, an amino acid found in almost all protein-rich foods. When PAH is broken — the disease is called phenylketonuria, or PKU — phenylalanine builds up to levels that damage the developing brain. Every newborn in most countries is screened for PKU with a heel-prick blood test within days of birth, because a lifelong low-phenylalanine diet prevents the otherwise inevitable brain damage. PKU is the original genetic-disease success story for newborn screening.

tagline: Lactase — digests milk sugar; most adults lose it (lactose intolerance)
function_friendly: Lactase is the enzyme in your small intestine that splits lactose (milk sugar) into glucose and galactose for absorption. All mammals make lactase as infants, but most humans stop producing it after childhood — which leads to gas, bloating, and diarrhea after dairy. The genetic variant that keeps lactase active in adulthood arose only about 7,000 years ago and is common in populations with a long history of dairy farming. It's one of the clearest examples of recent human evolution.

tagline: The cholesterol-making enzyme blocked by statins, the world's best-selling drugs
function_friendly: HMGCR is the enzyme your body uses to manufacture cholesterol from scratch, mostly in the liver. Statins — the world's best-selling drugs (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin, simvastatin) — work by blocking HMGCR. By lowering blood cholesterol, statins reduce the risk of heart attacks and strokes, and they're now prescribed to hundreds of millions of people. The discovery of statins by Akira Endo is one of the great stories of modern drug development.

tagline: The LDL receptor; broken in familial hypercholesterolemia
function_friendly: LDLR is the LDL ("bad cholesterol") receptor on the surface of liver cells — it pulls LDL particles out of the bloodstream so the liver can dispose of them. People born with broken LDLR have familial hypercholesterolemia: very high cholesterol from childhood and high risk of early heart attacks. Statins work in part by tricking liver cells into making more LDLR, which then pulls more cholesterol out of the blood.

tagline: A modern cholesterol target; injected drugs like Repatha lower it
function_friendly: PCSK9 is a protein that degrades the LDL receptor — fewer receptors means less LDL cholesterol is cleared from the blood. People born with naturally low PCSK9 activity have unusually low LDL and very low heart-attack risk, which inspired drug development. Repatha (evolocumab) and Praluent (alirocumab) are injected antibodies that block PCSK9, dramatically lowering LDL on top of what statins can do. Newer formats — small molecules and RNA-silencing drugs (inclisiran) — extend the same approach.

tagline: The structural protein of LDL — "bad cholesterol" particles
function_friendly: APOB is the structural backbone of LDL ("bad cholesterol") particles — every LDL particle carries one molecule of APOB. It's what allows the liver to assemble and the body to traffic cholesterol-rich particles around the bloodstream. People with rare mutations in APOB can have either extremely high or extremely low cholesterol depending on the variant, and blood APOB levels are sometimes measured as a more direct heart-risk marker than LDL cholesterol itself.

tagline: Breaks down acetaldehyde from alcohol; the variant behind Asian flush
function_friendly: ALDH2 is the enzyme that breaks down acetaldehyde, the toxic byproduct produced when the body metabolizes alcohol. A common variant inactivates ALDH2 in about 30–40% of East Asians, causing acetaldehyde to build up after even small amounts of alcohol — the cause of "Asian flush" (facial flushing, rapid heart rate, nausea). Beyond uncomfortable, the variant raises the long-term risk of esophageal cancer in people who drink anyway.

tagline: Protects red blood cells from oxidation; broken form causes favism and antimalarial reactions
function_friendly: G6PD is an enzyme that protects red blood cells from oxidative damage by producing a key reducing molecule (NADPH). Hundreds of millions of people worldwide — mostly men, since the gene is on the X chromosome — carry mild deficiencies. Usually harmless, but exposure to certain antimalarial drugs, the antibiotic dapsone, or fava beans can trigger sudden destruction of red blood cells (favism) in deficient individuals. G6PD deficiency is the most common enzyme deficiency in humans.

tagline: Salivary amylase; you have more copies if your ancestors ate more starch
function_friendly: AMY1A is salivary amylase — the enzyme in your saliva that starts breaking down starch the moment you take a bite of bread or pasta. Different humans carry different numbers of copies of the AMY1A gene, and populations with a long agricultural history (high-starch diets) tend to carry more copies. It's a clean example of dietary adaptation written directly into the human genome and visible by counting gene copies.

tagline: Burns calories as heat in brown fat; the molecular reason shivering and infant warmth work
function_friendly: UCP1 is the protein in brown fat cells that turns calories directly into heat instead of usable energy. Infants and small mammals rely on UCP1 for warmth, and shivering muscle activates a similar process. Adults retain small pockets of brown fat (around the neck and shoulders) that can be activated by cold exposure. UCP1 is a long-standing target for obesity research — the dream is to safely turn up calorie-burning thermogenesis as a treatment for excess weight.

tagline: Type I collagen — the most abundant protein in your body; bones, tendons, skin
function_friendly: COL1A1 is the main building block of type I collagen — the most abundant protein in your body and the structural fiber that gives bone, tendon, ligament, skin, and the white of your eye their tensile strength. It's woven into rope-like fibers that resist pulling, much like the wires in a steel cable. Mutations in COL1A1 cause osteogenesis imperfecta ("brittle bone disease"), where bones fracture from minimal force and the whites of the eyes can take on a bluish tint.

tagline: A keratin that builds the structural skeleton of skin cells
function_friendly: KRT14 is one of the main structural proteins that forms the internal skeleton of skin cells, working together with its partner KRT5 to build tough, rope-like fibres called intermediate filaments. These fibres act like a shock-absorbing scaffold, giving skin cells the mechanical strength to withstand friction and stretching. When KRT14 or KRT5 is mutated, the scaffold fails and the skin blisters at the slightest touch — the disease is called epidermolysis bullosa simplex.

tagline: KRT14's pairing partner; broken keratins cause blistering skin diseases
function_friendly: KRT5 is KRT14's pairing partner — the two keratins always work together as a pair, coiling around each other to form the rope-like intermediate filaments that give skin cells their mechanical strength. Mutations in KRT5 cause the same blistering skin disease as KRT14 mutations (epidermolysis bullosa simplex), because either broken partner collapses the whole scaffold and skin cells lose their structural support.

tagline: β-actin — the cytoskeleton thread inside every cell of your body
function_friendly: ACTB encodes β-actin, one of the workhorses of every cell's internal skeleton. Actin assembles into thin, dynamic filaments that give cells their shape, push their edges forward during movement, drive division when they split, and carry cargo around inside. Without actin, almost no cellular shape or motion is possible. It's so essential and so consistently present that biologists routinely use it as a "this should always be there" loading control in experiments.

tagline: β-tubulin — builds the highways cells use to move things around
function_friendly: TUBB encodes β-tubulin, which pairs with α-tubulin to build microtubules — the rigid highways inside every cell. Microtubules carry cargo from one end of the cell to the other, pull apart chromosomes during cell division, and form the rotating tails that let sperm swim. Cancer chemotherapies like paclitaxel (Taxol) and vinblastine work by disrupting microtubules, halting fast-dividing tumor cells in the act of trying to split.

tagline: Elastin — the springiness in your skin, lungs, and big arteries
function_friendly: Elastin is the protein that gives your skin, lungs, and large arteries their springiness — it stretches and snaps back through billions of cycles. The aortic walls bounce against every heartbeat thanks to elastin. Unlike collagen, the body makes almost no new elastin after early adulthood, which is why aging skin gradually loses its bounce and arteries gradually stiffen with age — much of what feels like "aging" of skin and vessels is the slow chemical breakdown of long-lived elastin.

tagline: Fibrillin-1 — elastin's scaffold; broken in Marfan syndrome
function_friendly: Fibrillin-1 builds the scaffolds onto which elastin is laid down, and it controls how the elastic fibers in your arteries, eyes, and connective tissue assemble. Mutations cause Marfan syndrome — a disorder marked by unusually tall stature, long limbs and fingers, lens dislocation, and most dangerously, a weak aortic wall prone to dilation and rupture. Famous athletes and historical figures with Marfan are often identified after sudden cardiac death; modern surveillance and surgery can prevent it.

tagline: Dystrophin — anchors muscle fibers; broken in Duchenne muscular dystrophy
function_friendly: Dystrophin is the anchor that links the contractile machinery inside muscle cells to the surrounding cell membrane and tissue. Without it, every contraction tears the muscle slightly, and over years the muscles waste away. Duchenne muscular dystrophy — caused by broken dystrophin — is one of the most severe and best-known childhood genetic diseases, affecting almost exclusively boys (the gene sits on the X chromosome). Modern gene therapies are starting to deliver shortened functional dystrophin to children with the disease.

tagline: The heart's main motor protein; mutated in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
function_friendly: MYH7 encodes the main motor protein of heart muscle — the molecular machine that converts chemical energy into the mechanical force of each heartbeat. Mutations in MYH7 cause hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, an inherited thickening of the heart muscle that can lead to sudden cardiac death in young athletes. It's one of the leading genetic causes of unexpected death in otherwise healthy people, and is the reason competitive athletes are sometimes screened by echocardiogram.

tagline: Titin — the longest human protein, the spring that gives muscle its bounce
function_friendly: Titin is the longest protein the human body makes — a single titin molecule stretches from one end of a muscle's contractile unit to the other and acts as a molecular spring that gives muscle its passive elasticity. Without titin, muscles couldn't return to length after stretching. Mutations in titin are now recognized as one of the leading inherited causes of dilated cardiomyopathy, a weakening of the heart muscle that can lead to heart failure in adulthood.

tagline: Lamin A — the inner skin of your cell nucleus; broken in progeria, the rapid-aging disease
function_friendly: Lamin A forms a meshwork on the inside surface of the cell nucleus — the "skin" that holds nuclear shape and helps organize the DNA inside. Different mutations in LMNA cause a startling variety of diseases: muscle and heart disorders, premature aging (Hutchinson-Gilford progeria), and abnormal fat distribution (lipodystrophy). Progeria is the most famous — children with it age 8–10 times faster than normal and rarely live past their teens, despite otherwise normal mental development.

tagline: Spectrin — the elastic mesh that lets red blood cells squeeze through capillaries
function_friendly: Spectrin is the elastic mesh that lines the inside of every red blood cell membrane, letting the cell flex and squeeze through capillaries narrower than the cell itself. When SPTA1 (or its partner SPTB) is mutated, red cells lose their flexibility and assume a spherical shape — the disease is called hereditary spherocytosis. The fragile spheres get destroyed in the spleen, causing chronic anemia. Removing the spleen often improves the disease.

tagline: The amyloid precursor — chopped into the plaques that mark Alzheimer's brain
function_friendly: APP is a long protein that sits in nerve cell membranes, where it has a normal role in cell signaling. Enzymes can chop APP into smaller fragments, including beta-amyloid — the sticky peptide that aggregates into the plaques found in the brains of people with Alzheimer's disease. Whether amyloid plaques *cause* Alzheimer's or are a byproduct of it is debated, but recent antibody drugs (lecanemab, donanemab) that clear amyloid do modestly slow disease progression.

tagline: The Alzheimer's risk gene; APOE4 carriers face higher odds
function_friendly: APOE is a small protein that carries cholesterol and other fats around the bloodstream and the brain. Humans carry one of three common variants — APOE2, APOE3, or APOE4 — and a single copy of APOE4 raises Alzheimer's risk roughly threefold, while two copies raises it about twelvefold. APOE2 is mildly protective. APOE4 is the single largest known genetic risk factor for common late-onset Alzheimer's disease, but it's a risk factor, not a guarantee.

tagline: Tau — forms the tangles found in Alzheimer's and other dementias
function_friendly: MAPT is the gene for tau, a protein that normally stabilizes the microtubule "highways" inside nerve cells. In Alzheimer's disease and a family of related disorders called tauopathies (frontotemporal dementia, progressive supranuclear palsy), tau goes wrong — it falls off the microtubules, becomes chemically modified, and clumps into the tangles that kill nerve cells from the inside. Drugs targeting tau are in active development alongside the amyloid-targeting therapies.

tagline: α-synuclein — clumps into the Lewy bodies of Parkinson's disease
function_friendly: SNCA encodes α-synuclein, a small protein abundant in nerve cell terminals. When α-synuclein misfolds and clumps together, it forms the Lewy bodies that are the hallmark of Parkinson's disease and dementia with Lewy bodies. Rare mutations or extra copies of SNCA can cause early-onset Parkinson's, and the protein has become a major drug target — multiple antibodies that try to clear α-synuclein are in clinical trials, in much the same way amyloid antibodies are being tested for Alzheimer's.

tagline: Huntingtin — the gene of Huntington's disease, dominant and currently untreatable
function_friendly: HTT carries a stretch of repeated glutamine amino acids, and the length of that stretch determines Huntington's disease. Repeats up to about 35 are normal; people with more than ~40 develop Huntington's — a progressive movement and cognitive disorder that strikes in midlife and slowly worsens over a decade. It's dominantly inherited, fully penetrant, and currently untreatable. Drugs that suppress production of mutant huntingtin are in active clinical development and represent one of medicine's most-watched experiments.

tagline: A free-radical sweeper; mutations cause familial ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease)
function_friendly: SOD1 is an enzyme that sweeps up reactive oxygen species, protecting cells from oxidative damage. Mutations in SOD1 cause about 2% of all ALS (Lou Gehrig's disease) cases and a larger fraction of inherited ALS. Tofersen — an SOD1-targeting drug approved in 2023 — became the first ALS therapy to address the actual genetic cause of disease, available to patients carrying the specific SOD1 mutations the drug is designed for.

tagline: The dopamine receptor blocked by every antipsychotic drug
function_friendly: DRD2 is one of the brain's main dopamine receptors. Every antipsychotic drug used for schizophrenia — from old ones like haloperidol to modern ones like olanzapine, risperidone, and aripiprazole — works in part by blocking DRD2. The same receptor is involved in the brain's reward and movement systems, which is why antipsychotics can cause both motivation/reward side effects and Parkinson-like movement problems as predictable downsides.

tagline: The serotonin transporter; the target of Prozac and other SSRIs
function_friendly: SLC6A4 is the serotonin transporter — a protein on the surface of brain cells that pulls serotonin back out of the synapse after it's been released. Every SSRI antidepressant (Prozac, Zoloft, Lexapro, Paxil) works by blocking SLC6A4, letting serotonin linger in the synapse longer. The same transporter is the target of MDMA, which forces serotonin to flood out rather than be reabsorbed — producing the rush MDMA users experience.

tagline: Brain-derived growth factor — supports memory and grows with exercise
function_friendly: BDNF is a small growth factor that nerve cells release to support the survival, growth, and plasticity of neighboring nerve cells. It's especially important in learning, memory, and mood. BDNF levels rise with aerobic exercise — a major part of why exercise reliably improves cognition and mood. Low BDNF activity is implicated in depression and several neurodegenerative conditions, and boosting BDNF is a long-standing goal of drug discovery in psychiatry.

tagline: Broken in Rett syndrome, a developmental disorder of girls
function_friendly: MECP2 is a protein that reads chemical tags on DNA to control which genes are turned on and off, especially in brain cells. Mutations cause Rett syndrome — a developmental disorder that almost exclusively affects girls (boys with severe mutations rarely survive infancy). Girls with Rett syndrome develop apparently normally for about a year, then lose acquired skills like speech and purposeful hand movement. Gene therapy approaches that restore MECP2 are in early clinical trials.

tagline: Silenced in Fragile X, the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability
function_friendly: FMR1 normally makes a protein essential for synapse function in brain cells. When the gene is silenced — usually by an expansion of repeating DNA at its start — the result is Fragile X syndrome, the most common inherited cause of intellectual disability and a frequent cause of autism. Fragile X mainly affects boys; girls with one broken copy are often only mildly affected, because their other X chromosome can compensate for the silenced gene.

tagline: The prion protein — its misfolding causes mad cow and Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease
function_friendly: PRNP encodes the prion protein. Normally harmless and found mostly on nerve cells, it can be triggered to misfold into a prion — an abnormally folded form that then converts other copies of itself, forming aggregates that destroy brain tissue. Prions cause Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease in humans, "mad cow" disease in cattle, and similar disorders. Prions are uniquely terrifying as infectious agents because they contain no genetic material — they are proteins infecting proteins.

tagline: The μ-opioid receptor — where morphine, heroin, and fentanyl all act
function_friendly: OPRM1 is the μ-opioid receptor — the molecular target where the brain's natural pain-relieving endorphins act, and where every opioid drug from morphine to fentanyl produces both pain relief and addictive reward. The same receptor in the gut slows intestinal movement, which is why opioids reliably cause constipation. Common variants of OPRM1 modestly influence baseline pain sensitivity and individual susceptibility to opioid addiction.

tagline: Rhodopsin — lets your eyes see in dim light; mutated in retinitis pigmentosa
function_friendly: Rhodopsin is the light-detecting protein in the rod cells of your retina that lets you see in dim light. A single photon striking rhodopsin triggers a chemical change in a bound molecule (retinal) that sends a signal to the brain. Mutations in RHO cause retinitis pigmentosa, a progressive blinding disease where rod cells gradually die — night vision is lost first, then peripheral vision. Rhodopsin was also one of the first GPCRs ever characterized and is the textbook example of the family.

tagline: The green opsin; missing or broken in red-green color blindness
function_friendly: OPN1MW encodes the green-sensitive opsin in your cone cells — one of the three color receptors (red, green, blue) responsible for daylight color vision. The gene sits on the X chromosome, which is why red-green color blindness is far more common in men than in women: men have only one X, so a single broken green or red opsin causes the trait. About 8% of men of European descent have some form of red-green color blindness.

tagline: The touch-and-proprioception sensor; its discovery won the 2021 Nobel Prize
function_friendly: PIEZO2 is a mechanical-force sensor in nerve cells — it opens when the cell membrane is bent or stretched, letting ions flow in and creating an electrical signal. PIEZO2 senses light touch, vibration, and proprioception (your sense of where your limbs are without looking). The discovery of PIEZO channels by Ardem Patapoutian won the 2021 Nobel Prize in Physiology. People with rare PIEZO2 mutations have an unusual loss of touch and a striking loss of body-position awareness.

tagline: Senses heat and chili-pepper capsaicin; the molecular reason peppers feel hot
function_friendly: TRPV1 is a temperature-and-pain sensor in nerve endings. It opens in response to heat above about 43°C, capsaicin (the molecule that makes chili peppers spicy), and several inflammatory signals. That's why chili peppers feel hot: capsaicin activates the same molecule that detects burning heat, and your brain can't tell the difference. Drugs targeting TRPV1 are in development for pain, and topical capsaicin creams are used to treat certain chronic neuropathic pain conditions.

tagline: Half of the sweet-taste receptor; why sugar tastes sweet
function_friendly: TAS1R2 pairs with TAS1R3 to form the sweet-taste receptor on the surface of taste-bud cells. The combined receptor is what makes sugar, sweet fruits, and artificial sweeteners taste sweet — they all bind to it. The same receptor explains why sweetness is universal across cultures: it's hard-wired into the receptor's chemistry. Different mammals have different taste sensitivities — cats, for example, lack a functional sweet receptor entirely, which is why they're indifferent to sugar.

tagline: One of ~400 olfactory receptors — the molecular basis of smell
function_friendly: OR1A1 is one of about 400 olfactory receptors in your nose — together they detect every smell you experience. Each receptor binds a small range of odor molecules, and the brain interprets the combined activation pattern across many receptors as a specific scent. Olfactory receptors make up the largest family of GPCRs in the human genome, taking up a remarkable ~3% of all your genes — a measure of how important smell has been across evolution.

tagline: Tyrosinase — makes melanin; broken in albinism
function_friendly: Tyrosinase is the key enzyme that makes melanin — the pigment that colors your skin, hair, and the iris of your eyes, and protects skin DNA from UV damage. Mutations that disable tyrosinase cause oculocutaneous albinism: people with the condition have very pale skin, white hair, and lack protective pigment in their eyes, often leading to visual problems and sun sensitivity. The amount and type of melanin tyrosinase produces also explains most of normal human variation in skin and hair color.

tagline: The doorway SARS-CoV-2 uses to enter your lung cells
function_friendly: ACE2 is normally an enzyme on the surface of lung, intestinal, and blood vessel cells, where it helps regulate blood pressure by inactivating a peptide called angiotensin II. It became globally famous in 2020 as the doorway SARS-CoV-2 uses to enter human cells — the virus's spike protein latches onto ACE2 to gain entry. The pattern of ACE2 distribution across tissues helps explain why COVID-19 hits the lungs, gut, and heart with particular force.

tagline: The marker of T helper cells; the same protein HIV exploits to invade
function_friendly: CD4 is a protein on the surface of T helper cells — the immune system's central coordinators that orchestrate the rest of the response to infections and tumors. HIV evolved to bind directly to CD4, using it as the doorway to infect and slowly kill the very cells that should be fighting it. The relentless decline of CD4 counts in untreated HIV infection is what leads to AIDS, and the CD4 count remains the standard marker for tracking how advanced HIV disease has become.

tagline: HIV's second handhold; people with broken CCR5 are partially immune to HIV
function_friendly: CCR5 is a second protein that HIV grabs to enter T cells — it's the "handhold" that complements CD4 for most HIV strains. A natural variant (CCR5-Δ32) that knocks out CCR5 is carried by about 10% of Europeans, and people homozygous for it are nearly immune to most HIV strains. The "Berlin patient" — the first person ever cured of HIV — received a bone marrow transplant from a CCR5-Δ32 donor, and the cure has since been reproduced in a handful of similar cases.

tagline: An MHC-I protein — your cells' ID badge to the immune system; matters in transplants
function_friendly: HLA-A is one of the MHC class I proteins that every nucleated cell of your body uses to display fragments of its inner proteins on the outside — like a constantly updating ID badge showing the immune system what's inside the cell. T cells inspect HLA-A and kill cells displaying viral or tumor protein fragments. HLA-A is one of the most variable genes in the human genome, which is why organ and bone marrow transplants need matched donors and why graft-versus-host disease is so hard to prevent.

tagline: The heavy chain of IgG antibodies — the workhorse of adaptive immunity
function_friendly: IGHG1 encodes the heavy chain of IgG antibodies — the workhorse class of antibody that does most of the heavy lifting in long-term immunity. After infection or vaccination, B cells make IgG antibodies that circulate in the blood for months to years, neutralize pathogens, and tag them for destruction. Every monoclonal antibody drug (those with names ending in "-mab") is essentially a manufactured IgG, targeted at a specific protein on a disease cell or pathogen.

tagline: The cytokine behind cytokine storms in COVID and sepsis
function_friendly: IL6 is a cytokine — a small signaling protein the immune system uses to coordinate inflammation. In a normal infection it helps marshal the response. In COVID-19, sepsis, and certain cancer immunotherapies like CAR-T, IL6 levels can spike uncontrollably, producing the cytokine storm: a massive systemic inflammation that itself becomes life-threatening. Tocilizumab, an antibody that blocks the IL6 receptor, is used to treat severe inflammatory conditions and rheumatoid arthritis.

tagline: Tumor necrosis factor — target of Humira, Remicade, and the anti-TNF biologics
function_friendly: TNF — tumor necrosis factor — is one of the immune system's most powerful inflammatory signals. It's central to the chronic inflammation of rheumatoid arthritis, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis, and Crohn's disease. Anti-TNF drugs (Humira/adalimumab, Remicade/infliximab, Enbrel/etanercept) are some of the best-selling drugs in history, transforming the lives of millions of people with autoimmune disease and changing how those diseases are treated.

tagline: A toll-like receptor; the immune system's tripwire for bacterial cell walls
function_friendly: TLR4 is a toll-like receptor — a tripwire that the innate immune system uses to detect bacterial cell walls. When TLR4 spots bacterial endotoxin (LPS), it sets off a strong inflammatory alarm to recruit other immune cells. Overactivation of TLR4 is central to sepsis, where bacterial products in the blood trigger runaway inflammation that ends up damaging the body's own organs. The discovery of toll-like receptors helped earn a 2011 Nobel Prize for the founders of innate immunity.

tagline: Master switch of regulatory T cells; broken in IPEX, a devastating autoimmune disease
function_friendly: FOXP3 is the master switch of regulatory T cells (Tregs) — the specialized immune cells that suppress immune responses and prevent autoimmunity. Without FOXP3, the immune system attacks the body's own tissues with no brake. Boys born with broken FOXP3 develop IPEX, a devastating multi-organ autoimmune disease that's fatal in early childhood without bone marrow transplant. Boosting Treg numbers via FOXP3-targeted cell therapy is an active area of research in autoimmune and transplant medicine.

tagline: Interferon-γ — the immune system's "rally the troops" signal
function_friendly: IFNG — interferon-γ — is the immune system's "activate everything" signal. T cells release it to tell macrophages to become aggressive killers, to ramp up MHC presentation on neighboring cells, and to coordinate defenses against viruses and intracellular bacteria like tuberculosis. People born with broken IFNG signaling are unusually susceptible to mycobacterial and viral infections, which is how doctors discovered just how essential IFNG is for keeping these pathogens in check.

tagline: Growth hormone — drives childhood growth; abused as a performance-enhancing drug
function_friendly: GH1 — growth hormone — is released by the pituitary gland to drive childhood growth. It tells the liver to release IGF-1, which in turn stimulates bone, muscle, and organ growth. Excess growth hormone in childhood causes gigantism; in adulthood it causes acromegaly, with enlarged hands, feet, and facial features. Synthetic growth hormone is used medically to treat growth hormone deficiency and famously abused in sports and bodybuilding for muscle gain and recovery.

tagline: Thyroid stimulating hormone — the brain's command to the thyroid gland
function_friendly: TSHB is one half of thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH), the brain's command signal to the thyroid gland. Released by the pituitary in response to falling thyroid hormone levels, TSH tells the thyroid to make and release more T3 and T4. TSH blood levels are the standard test doctors use to diagnose hypothyroidism (high TSH means a sluggish thyroid) or hyperthyroidism (low TSH means an overactive thyroid).

tagline: Prolactin — drives milk production after birth
function_friendly: Prolactin is a pituitary hormone that drives milk production in the breast during and after pregnancy. Levels rise during pregnancy and remain high while breastfeeding, since suckling triggers further release in a feedback loop. Prolactinomas — benign pituitary tumors that secrete prolactin — are the most common type of pituitary tumor and can cause infertility, irregular periods in women, low testosterone in men, or unexpected milk production. They're usually treatable with dopamine-agonist drugs.

tagline: Oxytocin — the bonding hormone of birth, breastfeeding, and connection
function_friendly: Oxytocin is released by the pituitary during birth, breastfeeding, sexual activity, and social bonding. It triggers uterine contractions during labor and milk letdown during nursing. Beyond reproduction, oxytocin shapes social attachment, trust, and pair bonding across many mammals — earning its popular reputation as the "love hormone," though the reality is more complex. The synthetic form (Pitocin) is widely used to induce or strengthen labor.

tagline: The estrogen receptor; target of tamoxifen and the breast-cancer endocrine drugs
function_friendly: ESR1 is the estrogen receptor — a protein inside many cells that binds estrogen and directly turns target genes on or off. About 75% of breast cancers depend on estrogen signaling through ESR1 to grow, which is why endocrine therapies (tamoxifen, aromatase inhibitors like letrozole, and the SERD fulvestrant) that block or remove estrogen are a cornerstone of breast cancer treatment. ESR1 also drives normal female reproductive biology and aspects of bone, brain, and cardiovascular function.

tagline: The androgen receptor; target of prostate-cancer drugs and a driver of male hair loss
function_friendly: AR is the androgen receptor — the protein inside cells that binds testosterone and DHT and switches male-pattern genes on. AR drives prostate development and most prostate cancers, which is why blocking androgen action (with enzalutamide, abiraterone, or chemical castration) is the foundation of advanced prostate cancer treatment. AR is also the central driver of male-pattern hair loss and is targeted by drugs like finasteride, which blocks the enzyme that converts testosterone to the more potent DHT.

tagline: Parathyroid hormone — raises blood calcium; the dial behind bone diseases
function_friendly: PTH (parathyroid hormone) is made by the four pea-sized parathyroid glands behind your thyroid. When blood calcium falls, PTH is released to pull calcium out of bone, retain it in the kidneys, and boost intestinal absorption — bringing blood calcium back up. Too much PTH (often from a benign parathyroid tumor) causes thinning bones and high blood calcium; too little causes muscle cramps and tetany. A modified PTH drug (teriparatide) is used to build bone in severe osteoporosis.

tagline: Corticotropin-releasing hormone — the brain's stress switch atop the HPA axis
function_friendly: CRH — corticotropin-releasing hormone — sits at the top of the body's stress axis. The brain releases CRH, which triggers the pituitary to release ACTH, which triggers the adrenal glands to release cortisol. Cortisol levels then feed back to switch off CRH, closing the loop. Chronic stress, depression, and PTSD are all linked to disturbances in CRH signaling, and the system is a long-standing target for psychiatric drug development that has so far been hard to translate.

tagline: Calcitonin and CGRP — lowers calcium and (via CGRP) drives migraine pain
function_friendly: CALCA is unusual — it produces two different hormones from the same gene through alternative splicing. One is calcitonin, made by thyroid C cells, which lowers blood calcium. The other is CGRP (calcitonin gene-related peptide), which dilates blood vessels and is a powerful driver of migraine pain. A new class of CGRP-blocking antibodies (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab) and small molecules (rimegepant) has transformed migraine prevention over the past decade.

tagline: Vasopressin / ADH — concentrates urine; the hormone broken in diabetes insipidus
function_friendly: AVP — vasopressin, also called antidiuretic hormone (ADH) — is released by the pituitary when the body senses dehydration. It tells the kidneys to retain water, producing more concentrated urine. People with diabetes insipidus either don't make AVP or can't respond to it, and they pass huge volumes of dilute urine and are constantly thirsty. AVP also constricts blood vessels, raising blood pressure, and is used as an injectable rescue drug in some forms of circulatory shock.

tagline: Hemoglobin β — carries oxygen in your blood; broken in sickle-cell anemia
function_friendly: HBB encodes the β-globin chain of hemoglobin — two β chains pair with two α chains to form the four-subunit hemoglobin molecule packed inside every red blood cell. A single amino-acid mutation in HBB (glutamate to valine at position 6) causes sickle cell anemia, in which red cells distort into rigid sickle shapes that clog small blood vessels. Other HBB mutations cause β-thalassemia. Both diseases are now potentially curable with gene-editing therapies that reactivate fetal hemoglobin.

tagline: Hemoglobin α — HBB's companion; broken in α-thalassemia
function_friendly: HBA1 encodes the α-globin chain of hemoglobin — the partner to β-globin (HBB) that completes the oxygen-carrying molecule. Each person has four α-globin genes (two from HBA1 and two from HBA2). Losing one or two causes mild α-thalassemia; losing three causes a more severe form; losing all four is incompatible with life. α-thalassemia is especially common in populations from Southeast Asia, Africa, and the Mediterranean — historically because carriers are partially protected from malaria.

tagline: Factor VIII — broken in classical hemophilia (hemophilia A)
function_friendly: F8 is coagulation factor VIII, a key protein in the blood-clotting cascade. When F8 is missing or broken, blood doesn't clot properly, and even minor injuries cause prolonged bleeding into joints and tissues. This is classical hemophilia (hemophilia A) — an X-linked disease that mostly affects boys and used to be lethal in childhood. Today, factor VIII replacement therapy and newer one-time gene therapies dramatically improve outcomes and quality of life.

tagline: Factor IX — broken in hemophilia B, sometimes called "the royal disease"
function_friendly: F9 is coagulation factor IX, another key clotting protein. Mutations cause hemophilia B — often called "Christmas disease" after the first patient described — which is clinically indistinguishable from hemophilia A without lab tests. Hemophilia B has historic resonance as "the royal disease": Queen Victoria was a carrier, and through her descendants the gene spread through European royalty, most famously affecting the Russian Tsar's son Alexei.

tagline: Prothrombin / thrombin — the central enzyme of the blood-clotting cascade
function_friendly: F2 is prothrombin — the inactive precursor of thrombin, the central enzyme of the blood-clotting cascade. Once activated, thrombin cleaves the soluble protein fibrinogen into fibrin, the mesh that holds a blood clot together. The blood thinner warfarin works in part by depleting vitamin K, which prothrombin needs to be made properly. A separate clotting-factor mutation called Factor V Leiden raises thrombin activity and is the commonest inherited cause of abnormal clotting.

tagline: The heart's sodium channel; mutations cause Brugada and long-QT arrhythmias
function_friendly: SCN5A is the main sodium channel that fires the electrical signal driving each heartbeat. Mutations cause several inherited arrhythmia syndromes including Brugada syndrome and one form of long-QT syndrome — disorders where the heart's electrical system can suddenly destabilize into dangerous rhythms. SCN5A mutations are one of the leading genetic causes of sudden cardiac death in otherwise healthy young people, and family screening saves lives by identifying carriers before a fatal event.

tagline: Cardiac troponin T — the blood-test marker doctors use to detect a heart attack
function_friendly: TNNT2 is cardiac troponin T — part of the molecular brake that controls heart muscle contraction in response to calcium signals. When heart muscle is damaged (as in a heart attack), troponin T leaks into the bloodstream. The blood test for cardiac troponin is the standard tool emergency room doctors use to confirm or rule out a heart attack within hours of chest pain onset. Inherited mutations in TNNT2 also cause hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

tagline: Myoglobin — stores oxygen in your muscles; gives red meat its color
function_friendly: Myoglobin is hemoglobin's cousin — a single-chain oxygen-binding protein that stores oxygen inside your muscle cells, ready to release during exertion. It's the reason muscle tissue is red, and the reason a steak is red rather than colorless. After severe muscle injury (crush injury, intense exercise, or rhabdomyolysis), large amounts of myoglobin can leak into the bloodstream and damage the kidneys — a dangerous complication of major trauma and certain extreme athletic events.

tagline: The chloride channel broken in cystic fibrosis — the textbook genetic disease
function_friendly: CFTR is a chloride channel on the surface of cells lining the lungs, pancreas, gut, and sweat glands. It moves chloride out, drawing water with it, which keeps the mucus on these surfaces thin and flowing. When CFTR is broken, mucus becomes thick and sticky, clogging the lungs and pancreas and producing the multi-organ disease cystic fibrosis. CFTR is the textbook example of a single-gene disease, and modern small-molecule drugs (Trikafta, ivacaftor) that rescue CFTR function have transformed life expectancy for most patients.

tagline: Spinal muscular atrophy gene; Zolgensma, the million-dollar gene therapy, replaces it
function_friendly: SMN1 makes a protein that's essential for the survival of motor neurons — the nerve cells that control voluntary muscle movement. Loss of SMN1 causes spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), a devastating childhood disease in which babies progressively lose muscle strength. Two transformative therapies now treat SMA: nusinersen (an antisense oligonucleotide injection) and Zolgensma (a one-time gene therapy that delivers a working copy of SMN1, famously the most expensive single-dose drug ever approved).

tagline: A copper exporter; broken in Wilson's disease, a treatable cause of liver and brain damage
function_friendly: ATP7B is a copper exporter in liver cells — its job is to load excess copper into bile for elimination. Broken ATP7B causes Wilson's disease: copper accumulates in the liver, brain, and eyes, producing liver failure, movement disorders, and psychiatric symptoms in young adults. A diagnostic clue is copper deposits visible as Kayser-Fleischer rings around the cornea. Wilson's is one of the few inherited neurodegenerative diseases that's effectively treatable — with drugs that bind and remove excess copper.

tagline: The iron-overload gene; broken form (hemochromatosis) is one of the commonest genetic diseases in Europeans
function_friendly: HFE is involved in sensing iron stores and signaling the body to absorb less iron when stores are full. A common mutation (C282Y) disrupts this signal, causing chronic iron overload — hereditary hemochromatosis. Untreated, excess iron deposits in the liver, heart, pancreas, and joints, causing cirrhosis, diabetes, heart failure, and arthritis. Hemochromatosis is one of the commonest inherited diseases in people of European descent, and treatment is simple: regular blood removal (therapeutic phlebotomy).

tagline: Neurofibromin — broken in neurofibromatosis, the disease of café-au-lait skin spots and benign tumors
function_friendly: NF1 makes neurofibromin, a tumor suppressor in the same growth-signaling pathway as KRAS. Inherited mutations cause neurofibromatosis type 1 — a disease that produces café-au-lait skin spots, freckling in skin folds, benign nerve sheath tumors (neurofibromas) on and under the skin, and an increased risk of certain cancers. About 1 in 3,000 people are born with NF1. A newer drug (selumetinib) shrinks the otherwise inoperable plexiform neurofibromas some patients develop.
